role_summary: First chairman of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (April 1960 - October 1961, founded at Shaw University in Raleigh); civil rights organizer in Nashville and Atlanta; later four-term mayor of Washington, D.C. (1979-1991, 1995-1999).
ai_reading: Barry preceded Charles F. McDew (entry 16) as SNCC's first chairman; the McDew catalog page references this transition directly. He is also referenced obliquely in the Courtland Cox (entry 24) and other SNCC-leadership catalog pages but is not himself a Civil Rights History Project interviewee. His standalone catalog page exists for searchability and to anchor the SNCC chairmanship lineage (Barry -> McDew -> Lewis -> Carmichael -> Brown -> Hutchings, the last of whom is catalog entry 98). The compression of Barry's SNCC tenure (eighteen months) and his subsequent four-decade D.C. political career means his cultural placement is dominated by the latter, but the corpus reads him through the former.
biographical_paragraph: Barry was born March 6, 1936 in Itta Bena, Mississippi to sharecropping parents who relocated to Memphis when he was eight. He completed a B.S. at LeMoyne College and a master's in chemistry at Fisk; while in graduate study at Fisk in 1960 he was elected the first national chairman of SNCC at the organization's founding conference at Shaw University in Raleigh, North Carolina. He served until October 1961, when Charles F. McDew succeeded him. He moved to Washington, D.C. in 1965 to direct SNCC's regional office there and remained in the city for the rest of his life; he was elected to the D.C. school board in 1971, the city council in 1974, and the mayoralty in 1978. He died November 23, 2014 in Washington at seventy-eight.

sources.title: Marion Barry Jr. with Omar Tyree, Mayor for Life: The Incredible Story of Marion Barry, Jr.
